coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nitrogen sulfur co doped carbon dots for dual detection of acidic and basic amino acids by fluorescence modulation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Min
Yang, Wenke
Liu, Qinze
Qiao, Congde
Li, Zhongwei
Yao, Jinshui
coredata.dc:description: This communication demonstrates an efficient one-step hydrothermal strategy for synthesizing N,S-co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) through molecular coordination between o-phenylenediamine and methionine. The obtained N,S-CDs exhibit dual-mode sensing capability for both acidic and alkaline amino acids via distinct fluorescence response mechanisms. In the spiked recovery experiments involving serum and urine with complex matrices, the sensor exhibited a recovery rate ranging from 95% to 105.09%, with a relative standard deviation consistently below 5%. This demonstrates its remarkable recognition capability in complex matrices. This work establishes a cost-effective amino acid detection platform, offering new opportunities for bioimaging and clinical diagnostic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual detection
Acidic amino acids
Basic amino acids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of N,S-CDs (inset: high-resolution transmission image of a single N,S-CDs); (b) Particle size distribution of N,S-CDs; (c) Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of Met, o-PD, N, S-CDs; (d) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of N,S-CDs (total).

(a) The effect of UV lamp irradiation time (0–30 min) on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs; (b) The effect of pH value (3–12) on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs. (c) Fluorescence emission intensity of N,S-CDs solution (containing twenty amino acids) and versus control (N,S-CDs solution).; (d) Fluorescence emission intensity of N,S-CDs @Asp solution (containing twenty amino acids) and versus control (N,S-CDs @Asp solution).

(a) The fluorescence quenching spectra of N,S-CDs with different concentrations of Asp added (the inset is the organized fluorescence emission point line graph); (b) The fluorescence intensity fitting curves of N,S-CDs with different concentrations of Asp added (c) The fluorescence quenching spectra of N,S-CDs with different concentrations of Glu added (illustrated as the organized fluorescence emission change point line graph); (d) Fluorescence intensity fitting curves of N,S-CDs with different concentrations of Glu added.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of nitrogen sulfur co doped carbon dots for dual detection of acidic and basic amino acids by fluorescence modulation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This communication demonstrates an efficient one-step hydrothermal strategy for synthesizing N,S-co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) through molecular coordination between o-phenylenediamine and methionine. The obtained N,S-CDs exhibit dual-mode sensing capability for both acidic and alkaline amino acids via distinct fluorescence response mechanisms. In the spiked recovery experiments involving serum and urine with complex matrices, the sensor exhibited a recovery rate ranging from 95% to 105.09%, with a relative standard deviation consistently below 5%. This demonstrates its remarkable recognition capability in complex matrices. This work establishes a cost-effective amino acid detection platform, offering new opportunities for bioimaging and clinical diagnostic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a promising class of nanomaterials for sensing applications [1] due to their tunable optical properties [2,3], excellent biocompatibility [4], and ease of synthesis [5]. Among various doping strategies, nitrogen and sulfur co-doping has been widely explored to enhance the photoluminescence (PL) performance and surface functionality of CDs, enabling their use in diverse analytical and bioimaging applications. However, developing CDs for the selective detection of amino acids remains a significant challenge, particularly in distinguishing between acidic and basic amino acids in complex biological environments.

Amino acids, as fundamental building blocks of proteins and key biomarkers in metabolic processes, play crucial roles in physiological and pathological conditions [6]. Among them, acidic amino acids such as aspartic acid (Asp) and glutamic acid (Glu) play an important role in cell–cell interactions, brain neurotransmission, and cell proliferation. Dysregulation of Asp and Glu may lead to glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, diabetes, psychiatric and neurological disorders, liver cirrhosis and cancer [7,8]. Basic amino acids, especially arginine (Arg), lysine (Lys) and histidine (His), are also crucial, Arg is involved in cell proliferation, wound healing and immune regulation, Lys is widely used in drugs and nutraceuticals, His may be associated with factors that improve metabolic syndrome, and have an impact on ion absorption However, excess amino acids can lead to hyperammonemia, metabolic imbalances, and toxicity, so accurate monitoring of acids and basic amino acids is essential. Traditional methods for amino acid detection, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], chromogenic reactions, capillary electrophoresis, gas chromatography (GC) [10–12], and mass spectrometry (MS), often require sophisticated instrumentation and time-consuming procedures. In contrast, fluorescent probes offer a green, rapid, cost-effective, and highly sensitive alternative, provided they can achieve selective discrimination between amino acids with similar structures.

Herein, we develop an economical fluorescence sensing platform capable of dual-mode detection for acidic/alkaline amino acids. Validation using spiked serum and urine samples demonstrates satisfactory recovery rates (95–105.09 %), confirming the reliability of this strategy in complex biological matrices. This work provides a promising approach for real-time monitoring of amino acid metabolism in biomedical diagnostics.

Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using 1,2-phenylenediamine (o-PD, 1 g, 308.3 mmol L−1) as the carbon/nitrogen precursor and methionine (Met, 0.7 g, 156.4 mmol L−1) as the sulfur source. Briefly, o-PD and Met were dispersed in 30 mL of ultrapure water under sonication for 40 min to achieve homogeneous dispersion. The mixture was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 130°C for 7 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was sonicated for an additional 20 min to ensure uniformity. The crude product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min (repeated three times) to remove insoluble aggregates, followed by filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate large particles, yielding a tan solution. The purified solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water using a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) for 48 h at room temperature. Finally, the N,S-CDs were freeze-dried under vacuum to obtain a powdered product, which was stored at 4°C for further use. Detailed experimental protocols are provided in the Supplementary Information.

Transmission electron microscopy analysis (Fig. 1 (a)) reveals the uniform spherical morphology of N,S-CDs with monodisperse distribution. High-resolution TEM (Fig. 1(a) inset) demonstrates well-defined lattice fringes (0.21 nm spacing), indicative of crystalline graphitic domains. Size distribution analysis (Fig. 2 (b)) confirms narrow particle dispersion (2.0–3.5 nm diameter), consistent with colloidal stability. FTIR spectral comparisons (Fig. 1(c)) between methionine (blue), 1,2-phenylenediamine (red), and N,S-CDs (black) elucidate structural evolution during carbonization. The disappearance of C=O stretching at 1700 cm−1 and concomitant emergence of carboxylate (–COO−) vibration at 1600 cm−1 suggest covalent interactions (e.g., amidation/condensation) between precursors. Weak N-H vibrations (3300–3030 cm−1) and complex bands (2000–1700 cm−1) imply partial protonation of aromatic amines to –NH3 + species, stabilized through charge compensation with carboxylate groups [13]. This dynamic equilibrium (–NH2 ↔ –NH3 +) aligns with surface charge modulation. The persistent aromatic C=C signal (∼1500 cm−1) confirms integration of o-PD-derived aromatic frameworks, while absent C-S vibrations (<700 cm−1) verify sulfur incorporation into carbon matrices [14]. XPS survey spectra (Fig. 1(d)) exhibit characteristic peaks at 285.16 eV (C 1 s), 400.87 eV (N 1 s), 531.23 eV (O 1 s), and 163.28 eV (S 2p), confirming elemental composition. The results of the elemental analyzer showed that the contents of elements C, H, N, O and S in N,S-CDs were 45.37 %, 4.31 %, 22.07 %, 24.92 % and 3.3 %, respectively. Doping of nitrogen and sulfur elements can effectively enhance the comprehensive performance of carbon dots, including improved water solubility, broadened luminescence range, enhanced stability and improved dispersion. As shown in Fig. S2, the characteristic π-π* jump peaks and surface state absorption bands in the UV–vis absorption spectra are a direct reflection of the synergistic effect of nitrogen and sulfur co-doping. Nitrogen-sulfur co-doping significantly narrows the energy band gap of the carbon dots, resulting in significantly better optical properties than those of the undoped carbon dots. High-resolution C 1 s (Fig. S3(a)), O 1 s (Fig. S3(b)), N 1 s (Fig. S3(c)), and S 2p (Fig. S3(d)) spectra collectively validate coexisting carboxylate (–COO−) and ammonium (–NH3 +) groups, whose electrostatic synergy enhances colloidal stability. These hydrophilic moieties (–OH, –COO−, –NH2/–NH3 +) not only ensure aqueous dispersibility but also enable charge-directed recognition for selective amino acid detection.

Fig. 2(a) demonstrates exceptional photostability under continuous UV irradiation (365 nm) for 30 min, with fluorescence intensity retaining > 90 % of the initial value, indicating minimal photobleaching. In Fig. 2(b), pH-dependent fluorescence behavior reveals optimal emission at neutral pH, with intensity reductions observed under highly acidic conditions. The fluorescence burst and recovery mechanisms of N, S-CDs under different pH conditions are shown in Fig. S4. Taking advantage of this property, we performed experiments on selective bursting of acidic and basic amino acids. Systematic evaluation of fluorescence response selectivity was performed by incubating N,S-CDs with 20 proteinogenic amino acids, including alanine (Ala), valine (Val), leucine (Leu), isoleucine (Ile), proline (Pro), glycine (Gly), methionine (Met), tryptophan (Trp), phenylalanine (Phe), glutamine (Gln), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), cysteine (Cys), aspartic acid(Asp), glutamic acid(Glu), lysine(Lys), arginine(Arg), histidine(His), tyrosine(Tyr) and asparagine (Asn) under optimized conditions (detailed protocols in Supplementary Information). As shown in Fig. 2(c) and 2(d), distinct fluorescence quenching was observed upon introducing acidic amino acids (Asp/Glu), while alkaline counterparts (Arg/Lys/His) induced characteristic fluorescence recovery. Notably, the remaining 15 amino acids exhibited < 5 % signal variation, demonstrating exceptional anti-interference capability.

Mechanistic studies revealed linear correlations between fluorescence quenching/recovery efficiency and analyte concentrations (Fig. 3 , Fig. S4), Complete linear regression equations, correlation coefficients (R2), and detection limits (LODs) are provided in the Supplementary Information. To validate practical applicability, spike-and-recovery assays in serum/artificial urine matrices containing 25 μM standard analytes demonstrated recoveries of 95 %-105.09 % with RSD < 5 % (Table S2), confirming anti-matrix interference characteristics for real-sample detection.

In conclusion, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) with yellow-orange fluorescence were successfully synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using 1,2-phenylenediamine and methionine as precursors. Optimization studies revealed that N,S-CDs synthesized at 130 °C for 7 h exhibited superior fluorescence properties. The dynamic fluorescence switching behavior originates from surface charge-regulated aggregation and electron transfer modulation. Leveraging this mechanism, a nanoscale fluorescence platform was developed for quantitative detection of acidic (Asp, Glu) and basic (Arg, His, Lys) amino acids. The platform demonstrated a linear detection range of 0–2 mM, with low detection limits of 1.51 μM (Asp) and 1.20 μM (Glu) for acidic amino acids, and 2.11 μM (Arg), 1.97 μM (His), and 2.32 μM (Lys) for basic amino acids. Practical validation in serum/urine matrices achieved 95–105.09 % recoveries with RSD < 5 %. These findings provide a theoretical foundation for designing multifunctional responsive nanomaterials and expand their potential applications in biosensing and environmental monitoring.

Min Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wenke Yang: Supervision, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qinze Liu: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Congde Qiao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Software, Project administration, Methodology. Zhongwei Li: Visualization, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jinshui Yao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2023ME152).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2025.138726.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots and their sensing applications, Trac-Trends in Analytical

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.Y. Wang, S.Y. Lu, The light of carbon dots: From mechanism to applications, Matter 5(1) (2022) 110-149.
F.M. Lin, C.Y. Jia, F.G. Wu, Carbon Dots for Intracellular Sensing, Small Structures 3(9) (2022).
